title: Tacos al Pastor Spit Grilled
description: Experience the vibrant flavors of Mexico with Tacos al Pastor, featuring thinly sliced pork marinated in a rich adobo of dried chiles and achiote, then slow-cooked on a vertical spit. The result is juicy, flavorful meat with crispy edges, perfectly complemented by sweet pineapple and fresh toppings for an unforgettable culinary journey.

ingredients.notes: trimmed and thinly sliced against the grain, about 1/4-inch thick
peeled, cored, and sliced into rounds, plus a chunk for the top of the spit

instructions.title: Prepare the Chiles
instructions.text: Place the guajillo and ancho chiles in a heatproof bowl. Cover them with boiling water and let them rehydrate for 20-30 minutes, until softened. Drain the chiles, reserving about 1/2 cup of the soaking liquid.

instructions.title: Make the Adobo Marinade
instructions.text: In a blender, combine the rehydrated chiles, garlic cloves, chopped white onion, achiote paste, white vinegar, orange juice, ground cumin, Mexican oregano, ground cloves, salt, and sugar. Add a splash of the reserved chile soaking liquid if needed, to achieve a smooth, thick paste. Blend until completely smooth.

instructions.title: Marinate the Pork
instructions.text: Place the thinly sliced pork in a large bowl or a resealable bag. Pour the adobo marinade over the pork, ensuring every piece is thoroughly coated. Massage the marinade into the pork. Cover the bowl or seal the bag and refrigerate for a minimum of 4 hours, or preferably overnight (up to 12 hours) for best flavor.

instructions.title: Assemble the Trompo (Spit)
instructions.text: If using a vertical spit (trompo) for an outdoor grill or oven, place a pineapple round at the base. Begin stacking the marinated pork slices onto the spit, pressing them down firmly to create a compact cylinder. Alternate with a few pineapple slices if desired, layering them between the pork to add moisture and flavor. Finish the top with a chunk of fresh pineapple.

instructions.title: Spit Grill the Pork
instructions.text: Set up your vertical spit on your grill or in your oven (if using an indoor rotisserie setup). Preheat your grill or oven to medium-high heat (around 350-375°F or 175-190°C). Cook the pork on the rotating spit for 1.5 to 2 hours, or until the exterior is deeply caramelized and crispy, and the internal temperature reaches 145°F (63°C). As it cooks, the outer layers will crisp up; use a sharp knife to shave off thin slices from the cooked exterior, allowing the inner layers to continue cooking and caramelizing.

instructions.title: Serve the Tacos al Pastor
instructions.text: While the pork is cooking, warm your corn tortillas on a comal or dry skillet until pliable and lightly toasted. Once the pork is shaved, finely chop the meat. Serve the chopped al pastor meat on warm tortillas. Garnish generously with finely diced white onion, chopped fresh cilantro, and a squeeze of fresh lime juice. Offer salsa taquera or salsa verde on the side for an extra kick. Slice additional fresh pineapple to serve alongside or on top of the tacos.

tips: For the most authentic flavor, seek out Mexican oregano, which has a more robust, earthy flavor profile than its Mediterranean counterpart.
When slicing the pork, aim for uniform thinness (about 1/4 inch) to ensure even marinating and cooking on the spit.

If you don't have a vertical spit, you can adapt this by grilling the marinated pork slices in batches on a regular grill, then chopping them. While not a 'trompo,' it still delivers incredible flavor.
Always taste your adobo marinade before adding it to the meat and adjust salt and sweetness as needed.
article:
## History of Tacos al Pastor Spit Grilled

Tacos al Pastor is a culinary marvel with a fascinating history, a true testament to Mexico's vibrant gastronomic landscape. Its origins trace back to Lebanese immigrants who arrived in Mexico in the early 20th century, bringing with them their tradition of shawarma – thinly sliced lamb cooked on a vertical spit. Mexican cooks ingeniously adapted this technique, swapping the lamb for marinated pork and infusing it with indigenous Mexican flavors. The adobo marinade, rich with dried chiles like guajillo and ancho, achiote, and spices, gave the dish its distinctive reddish hue and complex taste. Pineapple, a native fruit, was added to the top of the spit, its juices basting the meat as it cooked, tenderizing it and adding a sweet-tangy counterpoint. The name "al pastor" literally means "shepherd style," a nod to the original lamb shawarma, but it has evolved into a distinctly Mexican icon, beloved for its fusion of cultures and unforgettable flavor.

## Why This Recipe Works

This Tacos al Pastor recipe excels because it meticulously replicates the key elements that make the dish so beloved. The adobo marinade is the heart of the flavor, with the rehydrated guajillo and ancho chiles providing a deep, earthy, and slightly smoky base, while achiote paste delivers its signature vibrant color and subtle peppery notes. The addition of orange juice and vinegar provides the necessary acidity to tenderize the pork and brighten the overall profile, balancing the richness of the meat. Cooking on a vertical spit (trompo) is crucial; it allows the pork to slowly roast, caramelizing the exterior into crispy, flavorful bits while keeping the interior juicy. The constant rotation and the heat from all sides ensure even cooking and the development of varied textures, from tender to charred. The pineapple, cooked alongside, drips its sweet, acidic juices onto the pork, infusing it with a unique sweetness and helping to break down the meat fibers, resulting in an exceptionally tender and flavorful taco filling.

## Chef Tips for Perfection

To achieve truly exceptional Tacos al Pastor, start with the quality of your pork. A well-marbled pork shoulder (Boston butt) will yield the most tender and flavorful results. When slicing the pork, aim for consistent thinness—about 1/4 inch—which allows for maximum surface area to absorb the adobo and ensures even cooking on the spit. Don't rush the marinating process; 4 hours is good, but overnight (12 hours) is transformative, allowing the flavors to deeply penetrate the meat. For the trompo assembly, stack the pork tightly and compactly; this helps maintain its shape and ensures even cooking. When shaving the meat, use a very sharp knife to get those characteristic thin, crispy strips. Shave off only the caramelized outer layers, letting the inner pork continue to cook and crisp. Finally, don't skimp on the fresh toppings: warm corn tortillas, a generous sprinkle of fresh cilantro and diced white onion, and a squeeze of lime are non-negotiable for the authentic experience.

## Serving Suggestions

Tacos al Pastor are best served immediately, straight from the trompo. The shaved, hot, and crispy pork should be nestled into warm, pliable corn tortillas. For a complete and authentic experience, provide a generous array of garnishes: finely diced white onion, freshly chopped cilantro, and lime wedges are essential for that bright, fresh contrast. A vibrant salsa taquera or a zesty salsa verde is a must for adding an extra layer of flavor and heat. You can also offer thinly sliced radishes for a peppery crunch. Alongside the tacos, consider serving a refreshing agua fresca, such as horchata, jamaica (hibiscus), or tamarindo. For a heartier meal, a side of frijoles charros or a simple nopal salad would complement the rich flavors of the al pastor beautifully. Remember, the beauty of al pastor lies in its simplicity and the freshness of its accompaniments.

jsonLd.name: Tacos al Pastor Spit Grilled
jsonLd.description: Experience the vibrant flavors of Mexico with Tacos al Pastor, featuring thinly sliced pork marinated in a rich adobo of dried chiles and achiote, then slow-cooked on a vertical spit. The result is juicy, flavorful meat with crispy edges, perfectly complemented by sweet pineapple and fresh toppings for an unforgettable culinary journey.
jsonLd.keywords: Mexican, Mexican, authentic, traditional, lunch, Mexican, authentic, traditional, lunch, tacos, pork, spit-grilled, trompo, al pastor
recipeIngredient: 2.5 lbs (1.1 kg) Pork shoulder (Boston butt) (trimmed and thinly sliced against the grain, about 1/4-inch thick)
1 small Pineapple (peeled, cored, and sliced into rounds, plus a chunk for the top of the spit)
1/2 cup White onion (finely diced, for serving)
1 cup Salsa taquera or salsa verde (for serving)
